premises.premise: Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples exhibit zero resistance just above liquid-nitrogen temperature. 8, 9
Annealing at $450^{\circ}\text{C}$ in flowing oxygen depresses or destroys the superconducting transition of Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples.
Annealing at $450^{\circ}\text{C}$ in flowing oxygen slightly improves the superconductivity of Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples.
Tl-Ca-Cu-O samples prepared under conditions similar to those for Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples exhibit semiconductor-like behavior down to liquid-nitrogen temperature.

reasoning.step: The authors contrast the effect of annealing at $450^{\circ}\text{C}$ in flowing oxygen on Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples versus Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples.

Relying on [@premise-2], the authors identify that this annealing process depresses or destroys the superconducting transition in Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples (which otherwise have zero resistance above liquid nitrogen temperatures as noted in [@premise-1]).
Relying on [@premise-3], the authors identify that the same annealing process slightly improves the superconductivity of Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples.

Based on the contrast between the instability of the Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples [@premise-2] and the stability/improvement of the Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples [@premise-3], the authors infer that the addition of calcium to the Tl-Ba-Cu-O system stabilizes the superconducting phase against annealing.

Relying on [@premise-4], the authors note that Tl-Ca-Cu-O samples (without barium) exhibit semiconductor-like behavior down to liquid-nitrogen temperatures, contrasting with the superconducting behavior of the Ba-containing systems.

The authors combine the inferred stabilization effect of calcium, the specific annealing outcomes for both systems, and the non-superconducting nature of the barium-free Tl-Ca-Cu-O system to form the final conclusion.

inference_unit.conclusion: The addition of calcium to the Tl-Ba-Cu-O system stabilizes the superconducting phase against annealing; annealing at $450^{\circ}C$ in flowing oxygen slightly improves the superconductivity of Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples, whereas it depresses or destroys the transition in Tl–Ba–Cu–O samples, and Tl–Ca–Cu–O samples without barium exhibit semiconductor-like behavior down to liquid-nitrogen temperatures.
